The main claim is: I think Wikipedia is a useful format because it can include videos and illustrations (unlike papers) and it is more credible than blog posts.
The main claim is: A superior benevolent agent is one that is aligned and more capable than all non aligned agents.
In a similar vein, it seems difficult for an agent to figure out whether a 2nd agent which is not much worse than itself is more or less capable than itself.
The main claim is: It also seems that this difficulty is the main reason preventing us from outsourcing the issues of AI existential safety and Friendly AI to the AI systems smarter than us.
We also assume that various parts of this ecosystem tend to care overall about preserving continued ability to pursue goals and, therefore, about preserving the AI-ecosystem itself.
The main claim is: To actually see a general factor of doom, I would want to look at more than two questions.
If they were, it would suggest that a general factor of doom is driving people’s beliefs.
The main claim is: Influence: Once deployed, how much of the real world will the AI have influence over?
This would be the final barrier that can prevent a misaligned model from being “let out” in the real world.

The main claim is: I think it's possible that neither we, nor any future AI system, will manage to solve alignment.
But I think many of the obstacles to alignment also apply to cooperation.
The main claim is: There is nothing they can learn that we are fundamentally incapable of learning (even if we need technological aid to learn it).
The main claim is: It's still hypothetical, but it's starting to gain steam.
We're "putting steam into something" if we're putting a lot of time and attention into it.
